data.title: Token-Maxxing: An Arms Race No One Dares to Stop
data.description:
Author: Meng Xing, Partner, 5Y Capital; Source: LatePost

We took a trip to Silicon Valley and found that even the wave-makers are almost drowning in the waves.

On the morning of March 24, 2026, I sat in the audience at YC W26 batch Demo Day. When the fifth company came on stage to pitch, I decided to stop taking notes.

It's not that it's unimportant, but I realized that what I had written down might be outdated by next month.

This batch of over one hundred companies actually do highly concentrated work: about 80% are vertical agents, such as helping lawyers organize documents, helping customer service distribute work orders, and helping HR screen resumes.

If I had seen these projects last October, I would most likely have thought, "They're quite innovative." But the problem is, the world has changed in these five months. Claude Code has transformed from a more developer-oriented tool into an interface that almost anyone can use directly. With the release of Opus 4.6, the barrier to entry for vibe coding has been drastically reduced. Those vertical agents, before establishing business barriers, can now be built by an average engineer, or even myself, in a weekend; they have lost their investment value. YC's project cycle is three months; this batch, joining in December, plus the initial screening, are essentially "good companies" selected five months ago. Five months is enough time for several paradigm shifts to occur at the current pace of AI iteration. Back in 2012, when I started my first business and received a Fly Out from Y Combinator (YC), YC was practically unrivaled in the accelerator field, and the companies they selected often represented the "next direction." But the competitive landscape has changed, and in recent years YC seems to have reversed course, gradually becoming a lagging indicator. YC's batch system, from application, screening, induction, refinement, to pitching, has been operating successfully for over a decade in the mobile internet era. But this rhythm was designed for a slower world. Returning to the venture capital industry for the past year and a half, I've been to Silicon Valley about once a quarter; the last time was last October. In the past, every time I came here, I felt that things were changing very quickly, but this "quickness" was mostly perceived on a monthly basis. This time, it has to be measured in "weeks". One evening at dinner, a friend who works in post-training casually remarked, "I've noticed that Silicon Valley is starting to fall behind itself." [Image of a token-maxxing process] Six months ago, if someone had told me that tens of thousands of engineers at Meta were all writing code using competitors' products, I would have thought they were joking. But it's true. The entire Meta team uses Claude Code. This isn't a startup, nor some experimental team; it's a trillion-dollar company. Code security is disregarded, token budgets have exploded, leaderboards are booming, and the entire Silicon Valley is pouring money into AI without regard to cost. But what happens after all that? Let's start with code security. Six months ago, this would have been unimaginable, because code is a company's core asset. How could you allow an outside company's API to access it? Meta initially thought the same way. They internally developed something called myclaw to try and solve this problem. A friend at Meta told me they created a coding product, but it was "not user-friendly, nobody used it." After that, the company had to relax its restrictions: as long as it didn't involve customer data, anyone who wanted to use Claude Code could use it. Then, various departments started holding internal meetings on "how to become an AI-native organization," conducting training and performance evaluations. Code security and usage security, which were once taken for granted, were all relegated to the back burner; efficiency was the priority. For security reasons, Google prohibits most employees from using competitor tools like Claude Code or Codex, but DeepMind is an exception; several teams responsible for the Gemini model and internal applications use Claude Code. Google itself has also made efforts: they launched the internal coding tool Antigravity, and in February of this year, they announced that approximately 50% of the company's new code was written by AI. Even so, DeepMind is still using Claude Code. A key reason DeepMind dares to do this is that Anthropic has provided them with a private deployment; after all, Anthropic's inference and training primarily run on Google Cloud TPUs, and the two companies have this foundation of trust. But Meta and other tech giants don't have this relationship; they've truly disregarded code security. Everyone is betting on the same thing: maximizing speed first. Code security is just the first flag that falls; the second is the token budget. In the several AI-native startups I spoke with at Palo Alto, an engineer's annual token budget was roughly over $200,000. The number itself isn't surprising; what's surprising is that it means the AI ​​cost consumed by a top engineer is approaching their salary. It seems like companies are using AI to cut costs through layoffs, but in reality, the total cost may not have decreased at all; they've simply replaced human costs with token costs. Meta is the most extreme example in this regard. They created an internal token consumption leaderboard: whoever uses the most tokens is listed, and those at the bottom might be laid off. Meta employees are even vying for an unofficial title called "token legend." However, at the same time, Meta has conducted two rounds of layoffs this year, totaling tens of thousands of employees. On one hand, everyone is using Claude Code to boost token consumption; on the other hand, there are large-scale layoffs. These two things are not contradictory; they are two sides of the same coin. I visited a Series C funding company, and the technical lead showed me Slack. It was full of agents running—a dozen or so Cursor agents running in parallel in the background, with a Claude Code window for scheduling. The most prevalent anxiety among programmers right now is: if I don't know what my dozen or so agents are doing before I go to sleep, I panic. But has productivity really increased by that much? Since the end of last year, many CTOs of top inference engine and database companies have excitedly told me about "100 times more engineers" and "10 times more efficiency." What used to take 60 people a year can now be done in a week by two people plus Claude Code. At first, I was excited along with them, but later I calmed down and started asking myself: Okay, if efficiency improved 100 times, did the company's revenue increase 100 times? Or did the product line expand 100 times? Surely a "100-fold" improvement can't just mean laying off a lot of people, right? I didn't get a direct answer. The truth is, a 100-fold increase in efficiency only translates to a 50% or 1-fold increase in company revenue. Where does the difference lie? Nobody can say for sure yet. "With so many tokens, the company should have undergone a genetic mutation and become a completely different kind of company. But what exactly it will become, I don't know." A founder with a background in B2B sales told me that his team of 16 people, including two salespeople, achieved a $30 million ARR from scratch in 12 months, all thanks to AI coding. You do occasionally see cases like this. But most of the time, what I see are startups building more things, but these things lack product-market fit (PMF). Silicon Valley is currently popular for using Vibe coding to try 100 approaches to see which one works, instead of just trying 10. But who will be able to seize the next development trend? It's still hard to say. One of the most striking counterexamples came from within Anthropic. I asked a friend at Anthropic, "What's the most painful scenario for you when using agents?" He said it was oncall (instant response). A typical scenario for oncall tasks is: if Claude's API suddenly becomes slow, a model inference node crashes, or a user reports an abnormal prompt output, the oncall engineer needs to quickly pinpoint the root cause of the problem, determining whether it's a code bug, a computational allocation issue, or a model malfunction, and then decide how to fix it. Anthropic is the world's leading company in coding agents; this scenario couldn't be closer to their core competency, yet their internal oncall agent still doesn't work well. This is the reality in April 2026: the steam engine has been invented, but sometimes it's not even as fast as a horse-drawn carriage. The key is that everyone knows the steam engine will eventually run faster, so everyone is throwing money at it: code security is ignored, token budgets are overflowing, and leaderboards are booming. As for when the steam engine will actually outrun a horse-drawn carriage? Nobody knows, but nobody dares to stop and wait for that day. Because the cost of stopping might be greater than burning through the wrong tokens. Moreover, token consumption is unlikely to grow linearly. This reminds me of my experience working on autonomous driving: in 2021 in Shanghai, we achieved five consecutive hours of unattended autonomous driving for the first time. At the time, it was considered a major breakthrough. Before that, the test fleet might have gradually increased to 10, 15, or 20 vehicles; but after that inflection point, it quickly reached 100 or 1000 vehicles. Today's coding agents are in a similar stage. In 2021 in Shanghai, Didi's autonomous driving achieved its first continuous 5-hour unattended driving, a milestone event for autonomous driving in China. The picture shows Meng Xing, then COO of Didi's autonomous driving company, in conversation with Sebastian Thrun, the "father of Google's self-driving car," in 2021. METR is a California-based research institution specializing in evaluating AI coding capabilities. Last year, they proposed a metric: measuring how long an AI agent can complete a task with a 50% success rate (based on the completion time of human experts). When it was first released in March 2025, Claude 3.7 Sonnet achieved this in 50 minutes; by the end of 2025, Claude Opus 4.6 had reached 14.5 hours. Over the past two years, the doubling cycle of this metric has been compressed from 7 months to 4 months. Once the agent's reliability improves further, token consumption won't just increase by 50% annually, but will increase by an order of magnitude overnight. A widely accepted prediction is that by the end of this year, many companies (including major tech companies) will actually only need 20% of their workforce. After the xAI team collapsed, the rocket builders started building models. At a steakhouse in Mountain View, around 9 PM, a friend who had worked with Musk for a long time sat down across from me. We chatted for over three hours, and looking back, I realized he hadn't said a single good word about Musk. One detail: I asked him, "You've worked at xAI for three years, what's your daily routine like?" He said he'd basically lived at the company for three years, so his home wasn't very furnished, and he hadn't even bought a bed. He slept in one of those sleeping pods at the company, like in a youth hostel. I said, "Now that you have a huge amount of stock and have left, you should at least buy a bed." He smiled. xAI's workload is notoriously intense in Silicon Valley, but now about 90% of the early team has left. They have a departure group that's constantly adding new members. The trigger was Tony Wu's firing, which then triggered a chain reaction. As one insider put it, "Other companies might need six months to prepare for a senior management team leaving, but xAI only needed a month." Some people sensed Musk's dissatisfaction as early as last October, but didn't expect such a complete purge so quickly. Now Musk is bringing in people from SpaceX and Tesla to take over xAI; "the rocket builders are starting to build models." Musk's dissatisfaction stems from the fact that he poured countless funds and computing power into the project, yet Grok has never been able to reach the front lines. But why? This is a question I ask everyone I meet who comes from xAI. The answer is actually simpler than I imagined. A friend put it bluntly: the team is incredibly effective and works extremely hard, but the management style of manufacturing might not be suitable for large-scale model companies. Having worked in autonomous driving for eight years, I have some insights into this. Musk's past work with SpaceX and Tesla was essentially systems engineering: a long chain involving software, hardware, and the supply chain, each with room for innovation, but ultimately an end-to-end engineering problem. He excels at identifying key leverage points within this long chain and then compressing the timeline to overcome them. Rocket engine cascading and reusable landing are products of this thinking. But at xAI, what he does doesn't seem like systems engineering. He's doing three things now: first, building the world's largest GPU cluster (today, people jokingly say that xAI, originally a neo lab, is now more like a neo cloud, providing computing power to Cursor), then setting pulse-like deadlines for the team, and personally filming some product features. This is focusing on a few key points, not making a comprehensive plan. Those working in autonomous driving know that in the later stages, the core conflict becomes "who leads whom" among the software, infrastructure, and hardware teams. All three directions require CTO-level decision-making, but no one understands all three areas simultaneously. A better approach is for the founder, while not an expert in every area, to know how to balance resources and determine phased priorities—software first in one phase, then infrastructure in the next. This is called having a holistic plan. xAI's problem is the lack of this holistic plan; it's all about sprinting. If the pressure isn't too intense, intelligent people can actually self-correct; given time, each direction will find its own rhythm of collaboration. But Musk's extremely high-pressure management, coupled with insufficient overall planning, caused everything to fall apart under pressure. Each person in charge of each direction was prioritizing their own priorities; no one was coordinating the overall picture. One overlooked reason for SpaceX and Tesla's success is that Musk has essentially never encountered competitors of equal scale in these two industries; he's been competing against himself. But AI is different. AI is a fiercely competitive field where even OpenAI could be undermined by Anthropic. A co-founder of xAI said last year that there were two things he hadn't anticipated: first, the competition was so fierce; second, the opportunities for application innovation in the AI ​​era were so few, all being consumed by models. The rise of Anthropic represents the most dramatic reversal in the AI ​​industry over the past year. It has also completely changed the focus of the battle: a year ago, everyone was vying for C-end user numbers and video generation; now, the (temporarily) decisive battleground is B2B and coding. Of course, the xAI story is also a story of "what happens when money comes too fast and too much." I think those who left xAI today won't regret their decision to join; xAI is arguably the fastest wealth-creation myth in Silicon Valley. xAI went from raising billions of dollars in its first round of funding to becoming a $250 billion behemoth after merging with SpaceX, all in just one year. Almost every one of xAI's nine co-founders has become a billionaire, and its core engineers have earned tens of millions to hundreds of millions of dollars. Silicon Valley truly has too much money. If they were to start another business today, they would have the confidence to pursue their interests rather than chasing quick profits. Conversations with engineers reveal a strange unspoken understanding: everyone admits they don't write much code anymore, but pretends it's no big deal because they'll become AI-armed, eliminating those engineers who haven't embraced AI. Today, 80% of the core skills of software engineers have already been replaced by models. The only reason they're still around is because models occasionally make mistakes and require human supervision. But the act of "supervising" itself may soon be unnecessary. To take a more radical view: today's so-called "AI native organizations" sound very sexy—having each department streamline workflows, digitize AI-enabled components, and write them as skills. But essentially, it's about manually distilling oneself: you turn your abilities into machine skills, the company acquires your skills, and has effectively completed AI transformation. Whether to lay off employees because of this is a moral question. Meta is doing just that today. Although everyone is talking about token-maxxing today, you can still feel a pervasive sense of anxiety at the bottom of Silicon Valley. What surprised me even more was that this anxiety was spreading to the researcher community. Researchers are the elite at the very top of the pyramid. It doesn't refer to any general "researcher," but rather to those at large modeling companies (OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepMind, etc.) who are responsible for model training and algorithm innovation. The difference between them and engineers is: engineers "build things," writing code, deploying, and optimizing performance; researchers are further upstream, "thinking about what to build": proposing new training methods, designing model architectures, and running experiments to verify hypotheses. And now, even the work of researchers is being automated. This is what DeepMind is doing—using models to train models, which is also the AI ​​self-evolution that's been all the rage in Silicon Valley this year. This year, engineers are being phased out, and by the end of the year, researchers will also begin to be replaced. This isn't a new concept. Andrej Karpathy's auto research pioneered this approach, and today various AI scientist tools and harness frameworks are moving in this direction. However, most current closed loops only reach the "paper publication" level—AI runs experiments and writes papers for you, but ultimately, humans still make the judgments. Companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google are aiming for something more radical: they hope the closed loop will extend directly to model upgrades themselves, not just minor improvements, but allowing AI to find the next paradigm-level breakthrough on its own. If this can be achieved, it will truly replace researchers. Google DeepMind has been working on this internally for over a year, letting the model decide what experiments to run next, evaluate which path is more promising, and then follow that path—this is how the model trains its next generation. Moreover, researchers have a greater incentive to be laid off, for a brutal reason—because they are expensive. There may only be a few thousand researchers globally, with annual salaries often reaching millions, tens of millions, or even hundreds of millions of dollars. "The future scenario might be that 10 people do the work of 100 people, receive 20 salaries, and then 90 people lose their jobs." And the actual number of layoffs is much larger than the reported figures. Many companies are not cutting their own financial statements first, but rather their outsourcing service providers. This means that countries like India and the Philippines, which once provided customer service, data labeling, and financial back-office support to Europe and the US, may be the first to be impacted. The "service industry ladder" that some developing countries rely on to upgrade their economies may be being ripped away by AI. The entire Silicon Valley is watching Meta closely. If its experiment succeeds—revenue doesn't drop and efficiency truly improves—other large companies will quickly follow suit, and layoffs will go from isolated cases to industry norms. Moreover, layoffs have a cruel self-accelerating mechanism: initially, people are hesitant to lay off employees for fear of damaging morale; once it becomes the norm, layoffs happen faster and faster, with less concern for the consequences. However, while old positions are being eliminated, new ones are emerging. Many startups are starting to hire a new role called "AI builder"—combining the roles of product manager, front-end engineer, and back-end engineer. There are also hybrid roles combining data scientist and machine learning engineer, as well as content operators who integrate writing, advertising, and operations. Silicon Valley companies have a huge demand for these new roles, but the core problem is: nobody knows how to recruit them. You can't screen them out with resumes because the role didn't exist before, and the person's abilities might all be hidden in their own projects; you can't test them by having them write code on the spot because the core competency is a combination of "aesthetic sense + AI usage ability." So some startups are already doing this: automatically generating a simulated environment based on the employer's needs, allowing interviewees to complete tasks on the spot using AI tools. It's a bit like the old coding tests, but it tests something entirely new. When AI can do everything, the value of humans is shifting from "what they can do" to judging "what is worth doing and what shouldn't be done." One round of financing, two valuations: Nvidia wants to grab chips at every "table". We've talked about so many people being replaced—engineers, researchers, and financial professionals. But one role hasn't been replaced; instead, it's become increasingly like the behind-the-scenes boss in this reshuffling. This seemingly distributed world of innovation is actually extremely centralized at its core. That center is Nvidia. I originally thought the scarcity of GPUs had eased in the past year. There was indeed a period of relative calm. Around mid-2025, some neoclouds (new cloud service providers specializing in GPU computing power that emerged during the AI ​​wave), backed by Nvidia, struggled with fundraising, some experienced sluggish business growth, and some even sold themselves at that time. But this time, I found that scarcity has returned, and it's even more outrageous than before. A concrete signal: if you can stably provide an API service today, like Claude's API, achieving 99th percentile stability, you can sell it for two to three times the price of the official API. Following the surge in demand for Anthropic, API outages are becoming more frequent, posing a problem for many Agent products built on Claude. Previously, the business of providing Router services was based on the principle of "I'm cheaper than the official provider, so I get traffic." Now the logic is completely reversed: stability itself has become a scarce resource. A number of startups have made a lot of money from this, and mini versions of Coreweave/Nebius are springing up like mushrooms in Silicon Valley. Moreover, this time the computing bottleneck is not just a matter of GPU allocation. Elad Gil recently wrote an assessment I strongly agree with: the capacity expansion cycle for upstream memory manufacturers (Hynix, Samsung, Micron) will take at least another two years. This means that before 2028, no AI company can significantly differentiate itself by simply increasing computing power. Computing power constraints are objectively reinforcing the oligopoly in the large-scale model market—it's not that anyone isn't working hard, it's just that the manufacturing cycle in the physical world is that slow. The underlying power structure is clear: whoever has the cards is powerful, and who has the cards is determined by Nvidia. CoreWeave, Lambda, and Nebius, which went public today, all have Nvidia backing them. Nvidia's strategy is deeper than I previously understood. An investor in Reflection told me that when Neo Lab first came out for funding, it was doing coding. Then the founder met with Jensen Huang, who told him: "Stop coding, come out and create 'the American DeepSeek,' an open-source model for the US. I'll give you money and resources." Reflection then did a 180-degree turn. This led to some previously unseen structures in the US capital market: in the same funding round, two valuation tiers were offered. Investors with good relationships and early entry went into the lower valuation tier; established giants like Nvidia, with their deep pockets, and later investors were squeezed into the higher valuation tier. This structure has recently started to appear in China as well. But no matter how much Nvidia tries to control the allocation, it can't control something that doesn't exist. Protests against data centers are escalating across American society. Currently, approximately 100 data center projects nationwide are facing obstacles, with 40 likely to be cancelled altogether. Maine recently passed a bill completely banning data center construction. One town approved a $6 billion data center project, only to have half of its members voted to rescind it overnight; the sole purpose of the new leadership was to reverse the decision. The problem isn't a lack of computing power, not because products are bad or users are insufficient, but because the physical world can't keep up with the demands of the digital world. This is another level of "not keeping up." Silicon Valley's valuation system is being rewritten. Let's look at a number. The US GDP is approximately $30 trillion. OpenAI and Anthropic currently have annualized revenue run rates (ARRs) of around $30 billion each, meaning each company already accounts for 0.1% of the US GDP. If both reach $100 billion by the end of the year, plus cloud services and other AI revenue, AI will account for approximately 1% of the US GDP. From almost zero to 1%, it only took a few short years. This speed is unprecedented. But strangely, the faster the growth, the less investors know how to price the company—the valuation framework in Silicon Valley is collapsing in the face of such rapid growth. I had several in-depth conversations with friends in the secondary market, and one recurring term was "re-rationalization." In the past few years, the valuation logic for AI investments was based on future cash flows: it doesn't matter if you lose money today, I'm betting on your ARR (Average Return) three or five years from now. But now, this framework is flawed. The problem lies in the most basic valuation model: DCF (Discounted Cash Flow). Normally, with DCF, you predict cash flows for the next 10 years and then add a terminal value, which is the assumption that the company will continue to operate stably, packaging the remaining value in one lump sum. Typically, the terminal value accounts for 70%-80% of the total valuation. But two things have changed simultaneously: First, you can probably only predict 3 years instead of 10, because it's impossible to see what the industry will look like in 3 years (sometimes even 1 year); second, terminal value is even more impossible to calculate. It assumes the company will eventually operate stably, but if AI could disrupt everything at any time, the assumption of "stable operation" no longer holds. I discussed an analogy with a friend who works in secondary investment: companies not on the AI ​​front today are more like waiting for a "nuclear bomb"—you know they will be disrupted, just not when. Therefore, your evaluation focus shouldn't be "what if they aren't disrupted," but rather "how quickly they can respond when disrupted." This is a completely different valuation logic. SaaS was the first to be repriced by Wall Street. In 2023, Snowflake's return on investment, calculated based on free cash flow, would have taken nearly 100 years. Now, its valuation has been halved, and ServiceNow and Workday are following the same trend—this is just the beginning. Conversely, the only companies truly suitable for DCF valuation might be the leading large-scale companies, as their future seems to be steadily growing in a positive direction. They won't be "bombed," but rather focused on expanding their boundaries. In the past, startups would advertise "lower salaries, but stock options; they'll be worth a lot in the future." However, this approach presupposes that the company will still be around and valuable in 15 to 20 years. If that premise no longer holds, the most rational reaction from employees will be—"Don't give me stock options, just give me cash." This, in turn, will change the company's cost structure and financing logic. VCs are also suffering. In the past 3 to 6 months, almost every fund in Silicon Valley has invested in at least one neo lab, researchers from renowned AI labs who raised hundreds of millions of dollars with their ideas. But now, everyone feels it was a bit impulsive and expensive. But why did they still invest? Because if this company actually succeeds, its growth will be so fast that you'll feel the initial valuation was cheap. An investor friend put it bluntly: it's either zero to 100 or zero to zero anyway; rather than investing in an expensive Series A round and earning "hard-earned money," it's better to gamble on a ticket to a neo lab with unlimited potential. In the past, everyone thought that a $1 ARR meant exactly that, regardless of whether you were building models, applications, or infrastructure. But now, that equation has been broken. Vertical agents have the lowest ARR (around 5x), general agents have higher ARR (around 10x), and models have the highest (20-30x ARR, for example, Anthropic's $30 billion ARR, $800 billion valuation, 26.7x). A year ago, I thought multiplying the ARR by a uniform factor to calculate the valuation was sufficient, but today that method is completely wrong. Lime Trees and AI Assassination Lists Silicon Valley is experiencing a deep-seated security crisis. During my recent trip to Silicon Valley, I repeatedly heard friends seriously discussing the same thing: buying Bitcoin, building bunkers, and installing bulletproof glass in their homes—and they weren't joking. Recently, planting lime trees has indeed become popular in Silicon Valley because these trees have 4-inch thorns on their branches, and anyone attempting to climb over them will pay a price. The Wall Street Journal even reported on a $15 million "fortress mansion": a ring of lime trees planted in concrete flowerpots, behind which was a moat, and behind the moat was a laser intrusion detection system. The front door was a 3-inch thick solid steel plate with 13 bolts, and inside was a secure refuge room with a 2,000-pound heavy door. Even the landscaping was a defensive fortification. Companies providing residential security for CEOs saw their highest growth since 2003. This trend accelerated sharply, especially after the shooting death of UNH's CEO in Manhattan. Then, gunshots rang out at the doorstep of an AI mogul. At 4 a.m. on April 11, a 20-year-old boy wearing a Champion hoodie flew from Texas to California, carrying a kerosene can, stood in front of Sam Altman's $27 million mansion, lit a Molotov cocktail, and threw it inside. An hour and a half later, he appeared at OpenAI headquarters, grabbed a chair, smashed the glass door, and yelled at security, "I'm going to burn this place down and kill everyone inside." The FBI found a document on him titled "Your Final Warning." It listed the names and home addresses of several AI company CEOs and investors. Two days later, early Sunday morning, Altman's home was attacked again: a Honda sedan briefly stopped at the door, the passenger stuck his hand out the window, fired a shot into the house, and then fled. This was not an isolated incident. In late March, a large-scale anti-AI protest had already taken place in downtown San Francisco, with crowds holding signs that read "Stop the AI ​​Race" and "Don't Build Skynet," and giving speeches outside the offices of Anthropic, OpenAI, and xAI. Senator Bernie Sanders warned in Congress, "Humanity may really lose control of this planet." Talking with friends at xAI, it's said that Musk is also very worried about being shot, which is an open secret in the industry. The underlying fear is actually quite simple: if AI takes over most of production and humans are no longer essential participants in the economy, then all past social contracts regarding "how much you contribute and how much you should receive" become invalid. What remains is a minimalist power structure: whoever controls the GPUs and electricity controls everything. The social hierarchy isn't just widened, it's flattened: on one side, a tiny minority; on the other, everyone else. "Two years from now, the hottest campaign theme in the US presidential election will definitely be the relationship between AI and society. There might even be a Luddite movement in the AI ​​era." (Image of a PNG file: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7456285_image3.png) Inflation in the US remains severe. I've lived in California for many years and have never seen gas prices in the 7s. Just then, Citrini released its Global Intelligence Crisis report at the end of February, which simulated an economic crisis that could occur in 2028 due to AI's "over-success"... On the plane back to Beijing, I flipped through my notes from the past two weeks and realized that I had been writing the same word the entire time: "out of touch." YC couldn't keep up, Meta's code security guidelines couldn't keep up, xAI's management couldn't keep up, researchers couldn't keep up, computing power couldn't keep up, valuation frameworks couldn't keep up, and even society's psychological resilience couldn't keep up... to the point that Silicon Valley itself couldn't keep up with itself. But finally, I want to say that a friend from Anthropic mentioned that Dario Amodei said internally: "With the help of AI, cancer has been conquered in a sense. It doesn't mean it has disappeared, but rather that it may become a chronic disease that doesn't kill people. It's just that treatment costs are still too high, and widespread adoption will take time." I'm not sure if Dario's statement that "cancer has been conquered" is overly optimistic, but during my time in Silicon Valley, the most common startup direction we saw was AI4S and AI for Biotech. Many people from large modeling companies don't understand medicine, but they want to use AI technology to change the industry. In the past two weeks, I've seen so many companies "falling behind," which is indeed anxiety-inducing. But if AI truly makes cancer a chronic disease within a few years and accelerates materials science by twenty years, then this "falling behind" might be the biggest acceleration in human history. My child is two years old this year, and we might have a second child next year. I have absolutely no imagination to imagine what the world their generation will face will be like. But I hope that in the world they grow up in, there will be more people cured by AI, rather than more Molotov cocktails and gunshots raining down on the doorsteps of AI practitioners. In his 2008 book, *Cities and Ambition*, Paul Graham wrote: “While people in Silicon Valley have great respect for intelligence, the message Silicon Valley sends is: you should be more influential, which is not entirely the same message it sends in New York. Influence is certainly important in New York, but New York highly values ​​‘billions of dollars,’ even if you just inherited that money. But in Silicon Valley, apart from a few real estate agents, nobody cares about that. What really matters in Silicon Valley is how much impact you have on the world. People care about Larry and Sergey not because of their wealth, but because they control Google, and Google affects almost everyone.” Now, AI has pushed this atmosphere to a new level. LatePost columnist Meng Xing: Partner at Five Sources Capital and former COO of Didi Autonomous Driving. This is his first article in his AI investment observations; he will continue to update his investment observations on LatePost.
